dbloader.cpp

libdbloader_la_CXXFLAGS = -I/usr/include \

-I../../libsqlany \
@ODBC_CFLAGS@ \
@POSTGRES_CXXFLAGS@ \
@MYSQL_CXXFLAGS@ \
-I@SQLANY@/sdk/include \
`wx-config \
--cxxflags`

libdbloader_la_LDFLAGS = -L../dbinterface \
-ldbinterface \
-L../libsqlite \
-L../libodbc \
-L../libpostgres \
-L../libmysql \

-lsqlany \

@POSTGRES_LIBS@ \

-ldbcapi \
`wx-config \
--libs`

[/code]

 

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Baseline date and net due date mismatch in the Import...
entry.content:
Hi Kazuhiro,

Thanks — that clarifies the target state, and it's straightforward to configure. It's the same record we were discussing: whichever day-limit entry under Z120 is selected by the day of your baseline date (the 10th) is the one you need to change.

What to change

In Maintain Terms of Payment (SSCUI 102934), open that record and:

  • Clear all three No. of Days lines (including the "1" in term line 1) and leave the percentages at 0. With no period maintained, the item is due on the baseline date — that alone removes the +1 day you saw.
  • Make sure Fixed Day and Additional Months are empty on that record, and Default for baseline date = Posting Date. Otherwise the baseline itself moves, and the due date follows it. Reference: KBA 2926267

That gives you exactly Net payment days = 0 and Baseline date = Net due date.

One constraint to plan around

The Day Limit of an existing payment term cannot be edited in Public Cloud — only the values inside each record. So if Z120 has several day-limit records, you have to repeat the change on every one of them, otherwise invoices dated in another range will still behave differently. If the day-limit split has no business purpose anymore, it is cleaner to create a new key (e.g. Z121) with a single record, Day Limit blank, baseline default = Posting Date, no term lines, and reassign it in the supplier's company-code data. Reference: KBA 2926267

Holidays stay as they are

As mentioned before, the non-workday correction is a separate mechanism driven by the calendar, not by the number of days — setting the periods to 0 does not disable it, so the bank-holiday shift you already use continues to apply on top of the new due date. Reference: Shifting Payment Due Dates That Fall on Bank Holidays

Verify

Post one test invoice with a posting date in the changed day-limit range and check the Payment tab: Days 1 = 0 and Net Due Date = Baseline Date. Then repeat via F3041. Already posted documents are not recalculated, so this applies to new invoices only.

BR, Michal

entry.title: SAP Inside Track Munich 2026 | Call for Speakers 📢 #sitMUC26
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

On October 10th, the SAP community will gather for the 10th time (yey!) once again in Garching for a day full of learning, sharing, and connecting... and we want you to be part of it.

Whether you're deep into ABAP, building apps on SAP BTP, working with Fiori, S/4HANA, or Analytics, exploring DevOps in SAP, or shaping business processes, driving change in projects, or navigating transformation from a consulting perspective, your insights matter. Our audience comes from all corners of the SAP universe: developers, consultants, architects, product experts, and curious newcomers. That’s why we’re looking for diverse session ideas: technical deep dives, lessons learned from projects, open-source initiatives, or even side projects that others can learn from. It doesn’t have to be perfect or polished. If it’s real and relevant to the community, it belongs on stage.

Important dates and submission process:

  • Call opens: July 19st
  • Submission deadline: August 30th
  • Speaker selection: by September 13th
  • Please submit your proposal via email: speakers@sitmuc.com

There are various session types for your submissions:

  • Short Presentation (20 min)
  • Presentation (40 min)
    you will present a topic to the audience demos, theory, live coding, or anything else that suits your expertise
  • Marketplace
    you will share your topic in a “marketplace” breakout session where everyone is welcome to walk around, visit the various stands, and interact

What we’re looking for:

  • On-site only we’ll be together in person!
  • No marketing pitches – just genuine value for the community

We’d love to hear your ideas whether you’re a seasoned speaker or presenting for the very first time. Let’s make #sitMUC26 another unforgettable community event.

If you're unsure about your topic, format, or how to present it no worries! Just drop us an email at speakers@sitmuc.com, we're happy to help. 

Let’s build this day together. Bring your voice, your story, your topic. We can’t wait to hear it!

Best regards,
sitMUC Orga team

entry.title: Re: how to add the slip issue number into the work order
entry.content: @ MAH_SAP123 If the requirement is to record issue slip numbers at the individual material/component level within a work order, and since a single work order can involve multiple issue slips, it may be inadequate to store the number only in the work order header. Alternatives include: 1) A component-level custom field to maintain issue slip numbers directly with each material/component, providing clarity. 2) Utilizing material document numbers for traceability between work order components and issue slips. 3) Creating a custom table to manage many-to-many relationships between work orders, components, and issue slips for greater flexibility.

entry.title: Re: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 2 — Roll & Move
entry.content:
Start-Problems

The type: module made a problem.
To keep this setting, the Exports-Statement in game-service.js had to be changed.

module.exports = class GameService extends cds.ApplicationService { ..

export default class GameService extends cds.ApplicationService { ...

 

Tasks


1. Your srv/game-service.js handler implementations (paste as a JS code block)

 

import cds from '@sap/cds'

export default class GameService extends cds.ApplicationService {
  async init() {

    const { Players, BoardSquares } = this.entities

    // ── startTurn ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Waiting]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
    // @TO: #Playing      →  CAP updates status after this returns
    // No business logic needed — CAP's @flow.status handles the transition.
    this.on('startTurn', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Intentionally empty: the @from/@to annotations do all the work.
    })

    // ── rollDice ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Playing]  →  CAP validates status before this runs
    // @TO: #Moving       →  CAP updates status after this returns
    this.on('rollDice', 'Players', async (req) => {
      const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
      if (!player) return req.error(404, 'Player not found')

      const roll = Math.ceil(Math.random() * 6)
      let newPosition = player.position + roll

      // M.L. 19.07.2026 war nur zum Test, um Würfeln zu umgehen. 
      //let newPosition = player.position + 1

      // Can't overshoot square 100 — must land exactly
      if (newPosition > 100) {
        await UPDATE(Players).set({ lastRoll: roll }).where({ ID: player.ID })
        const needed = 100 - player.position
        return req.reject(409, `Need exactly ${needed} to finish. Rolled ${roll}. No move.`)
      }

      // Resolve snake or ladder on the new square
      const square = await SELECT.one.from(BoardSquares).where({ square: newPosition })
      let finalPosition = newPosition
      let event = null

      if (square?.ladderTo) {
        finalPosition = square.ladderTo
        event = { type: 'ladder', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
      } else if (square?.snakeTo) {
        finalPosition = square.snakeTo
        event = square.isDoubleHead
          ? { type: 'doubleSnake', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
          : { type: 'snake', 'from': newPosition, 'to': finalPosition }
      }

      // Persist position and last roll
      await UPDATE(Players)
        .set({ position: finalPosition, lastRoll: roll })
        .where({ ID: player.ID })

      // Emit domain event so subscribers (Week 3) can react
      if (event) await this.emit('BoardEvent', { playerID: player.ID, ...event })

      // Use req.reply() — not return — to pin the response value before CAP's
      // @flow.status after-handler runs. A plain `return` gets cleared by the
      // flow machinery, producing HTTP 204 instead of the expected 200 + body.
      req.reply({ roll, position: finalPosition, event: event?.type ?? 'normal' })
    })

    // ── confirmMove ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  normal end of a turn, hands off to next player (Week 3)
    // @TO: #Waiting     →  player waits for their next turn
    this.on('confirmMove', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Week 3 will add turn rotation here.
      // For now: just let CAP apply @TO: #Waiting
    })

    // ── blockPlayer ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player lands on a double-headed snake
    // @TO: #Blocked     →  player skips their next turn
    this.on('blockPlayer', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // Business logic: the position was already updated in rollDice.
      // This action just transitions state — CAP handles the @TO: #Blocked update.
      req.info('Player is blocked and will skip their next turn.')
    })

    // ── unblockPlayer ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Blocked]   →  called at the start of a blocked player's next turn
    // @TO: $flow.previous →  CAP restores the status to whatever it was before #Blocked
    this.on('unblockPlayer', 'Players', async (req) => {
      // CAP's $flow.previous does the work — no code needed here.
      // The player's status returns to #Waiting automatically.
    })

    // ── winGame ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // @from: [#Moving]  →  called when player.position === 100
    // @TO: #Finished    →  player has won
    this.on('winGame', 'Players', async (req) => {
      const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
      if (!player) return req.error(404, 'Player not found')


      // M.L. 19.07.2026 Idea from waseem1713 
      if (player.position !== 100) {
        return req.reject(400, `Player must be on square 100 to win. Current position: ${player.position}`)
      } 

      // Mark the session as finished with this player as winner
      await UPDATE('snakeladder.GameSessions')
        .set({
          finishedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
          winner_ID: player.ID
        })
        .where({ ID: player.session_ID })

      await this.emit('GameWon', { playerID: player.ID, sessionID: player.session_ID })
    })


    this.on('skipTurn', 'Players', async (req) => {
      const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
      await UPDATE(Players)
        .set({ turnsBlocked: (player.turnsBlocked || 0) + 1 })
        .where({ ID: player.ID })
    })




    return super.init()
  }
}

2. A screenshot showing the HTTP 409 response when calling rollDice in the wrong state

 



3. A screenshot or OData response showing your player's position after landing on a snake or ladder



4. One sentence: what happens if you call winGame on a player whose position is not 100? How does your implementation handle it?

The Turn-Status is set to “Finished“, without any check.

 

To Intercept that i added a checker (like waseem1713)

if (player.position !== 100) {

        return req.reject(400, `Player must be on square 100 to win. Current position: ${player.position}`)

      }



Stretch-Goal (Skip Turn)

Description:

The Flow-Status is set to previous via annotation.
Additional a counter is set via event-handling on Skip-Turn

Process:

Blocking

 

Skip Turn

entry.title: Hotfix Collection Application Changes after the 2608 Upgrade of the SAP ERP Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Introduction

In my recent blog Getting Ready for the 2608 Upgrade of the SAP Cloud ERP Public Edition, I discussed the major changes in upgrade procedure, and the Hotfix Collection (HFC) application pattern change. However, when I discussed the topic with my colleagues, I noticed it is still confusing to understand how the HFC application is done. Therefore, I am going to explain the topic further in this short blog.

Background and Motivation

One loud feedback we heard from our customers is that too many weekend maintenance work in the SAP ERP Cloud Public Edition. We aim to reduce this disruption to customer’s weekend work and harmonize the cloud maintenance operations at the same weekend for multiple SAP cloud products. This becomes the motivation of introducing the new HFC application process.

New HFC Application Process from September 2026

In the SAP Document SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, 3-System Landscape Upgrade & Maintenance Schedule, it has one slide titled “Maintenance Calendar”.  I screenshot a portion of that busy table for our discussion as seen below.

HFC Application Schedule after 2608 Upgrade

 Let me focus on the Column October in this blog, but you can apply our discussion to other months. We assume four weekends per month.  Every weekend, we have four hours Contractual Maintenance Periods (CMP).  This is still in place as part of our contract.  Until now, we used most of it. However, from now on, we will try to use as less as possible to reduce the disruption.

Standard Contractual Maintenance Periods (CMP)

First, we pretty much moved the old infrastructure related maintenance to be online. Therefore, there is no more dark-blue weekend.  Then, we combined all the work needs downtime into one called the Standard Maintenance Weekend, shown as dark-green like October 10-11.  Subsequently, we have one more row of light-green on Oct. 17-18, called the Online Software Change Weekend. Of course, we still have the Optional Maintenance Weekend (not shown) on a needed basis, as part of the Contractual Maintenance Periods.  All of these weekend work are system maintenance related.

Next comes the HFC itself. Before moving on, I talk about a new technique we use to apply HFC: When applying HFC to the P tenant, the Blue Green Deployment Method (see my blog) is used. This creates a “near zero downtime maintenance” (nZDM).  The downtime is less than 1 min on average to switch the database schema from the old content version to the new content version. To users, this is a no downtime applying a HFC.  In contrast, for non-P tenants, we still use the conventional method and it takes 30 to 60 min downtime to apply a HFC.

In addition, we changed the biweekly HFC release schedule to once a month, such as HFC05 for October, HFC06 for November, etc. One week later, we release a critical bug fix HFCxx+, no new features are added. By looking at the October column closely, we can observe:

  1. The HFC05 is only applied to the T and other tenants. This creates a one-week Preview window to check out the new features in the T tenant.
  2. For the P and D tenants, we only apply HFC05+, aka the bug free version of HFC05 to it.  This makes P and D tenants more stable.
  3. For the P tenant, it gets HFC05+ without a downtime.
  4. Only two weekends are impacted by HFC application. Another two weekends are free of maintenance, unless there is an urgent repair needs to be carried out.

When we focus on the entire month of P tenant, we notice that it only receives one downtime (dark-green) weekend out of four!

In summary, we can see the benefits of the new HFC application process as following:

  • The HFC is released monthly, and the D/P Tenants receive a bug free HFC
  • Creating a one-week preview window on T tenant for each HFC content, before the D/P Tenants receive the same.  This is consistent with the major upgrade (two-week preview window).
  • The downtime disruption to customer’s P tenant is drastically reduced (4 vs. 1 in a month):
    • Only one downtime weekend each month for Standard Maintenance.
    • The HFCxx+ is applied without a downtime.
    • Two weekends are free of any maintenance activities.
  • The downtime Standard Maintenance Window is harmonized with other SAP Cloud Products to synchronize the maintenance schedule.

 

Summary

By listening to our customers, we took the advantage of new maintenance techniques and regrouped maintenance activities, the impact of applying HFC to customers are drastically reduced after the 2608 Upgrade.

 

References

  • My blog: Understanding Upgrading and Patching Processes of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  • My blog: Getting Ready for the 2608 Upgrade of the SAP Cloud ERP Public Edition
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, 3-System Landscape Upgrade & Maintenance Schedule (February 19, 20...
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Software Lifecycle Management for Customers (September 3, 2025)

entry.title: Unexpected Ariba Installation Provisioned on Account – Request for Investigation and Decommissioning
entry.content:
An SAP Ariba installation has been provisioned under our SAP for Me account, although we did not request this service and do not believe we have any Ariba entitlements associated with our contracts. Could SAP Cloud Operations please investigate why this Ariba installation was provisioned on our account? we request that the installation be decommissioned.


--- Steps to Reproduce ---

1. Log in to SAP for Me.2. Navigate to Services & Support > Systems & Provisioning > Systems.3. Search for Installation DIGITS.4. Open the installation details.5. Observe that the installation exists under our account.6. Observe that the "Created By" field is blank and no requester or entitlement details are visible



--- Issue Date (UTC) ---
DIGITS-07-11 20:00:00

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Unexpected Ariba Installation Provisioned on Account...
entry.content:
I understand you have some questions regarding a new Ariba installation which was created under your customer number 1685040.

There are a couple different ways installation numbers get created which I will explain. I will also provide information on who to contact if you need further details on who/why an installation was created by SAP.

 

To view 'who' created your company's various installations, go to https://me.sap.com/app/installations and find the "Created By" column. 

On-premise installation numbers are usually requested by your company's S-users in SAP for Me using the self-service option according to KBA 0001773826. Creating this way will show the S-user ID in the "Created By" column.

Cloud installation numbers usually start with 90* and are created by "SAP". 

If the "Created By" doesn't list a S-user ID and shows Created By "SAP", you can assume the installation was created by SAP through some internal process or when a new contract (new product) is added to the account -- this includes On Premise as well as Cloud.

This issue is documented in KBA 3564759 - Notification for a New Installation Number - SAP for Me 

 

When a new installation is created (whether it is an on-premise installation requested by an S-user or a cloud installation automatically generated from your contract), an email notification is automatically sent to all S-users who hold the authorization object "Manage Installations."

If you need clarification regarding who created the installation or why it was created, please contact SAP Order Management using SAP KBA 1660069, or reach out directly to your responsible SAP Account Manager as below:

They are responsible for contractual matters and can provide details about the purchased product and why the installation number has been created.

entry.title: China Audit Reports (CADE) in SAP S/4HANA — What It Is and Who Needs It
entry.content:
What Is CADE?

CADE (China Audit Data Extraction, 中国审计数据摘取) is SAP's solution for generating structured financial data files per Chinese national standard GB/T 24589.01-2010. It produces XML output covering four modules: Common Information, General Ledger (GL), Accounts Payable/Receivable (APAR), and Fixed Assets (AA).

Supported versions: S/4HANA Public Cloud 2011+ | S/4HANA Private Cloud / On-Premise 1709+

Who Needs It — Broader Than You Might Think

GB/T 24589 was originally mandated by the China National Audit Office (CNAO) for the Golden Audit (金审工程) program — enterprises nominated by the CNAO must submit financial data in this format for a specified period.

But in practice, the reach goes further. Professional audit firms — including Big 4 and local CPA firms — increasingly request data in this format during annual statutory business audits. It gives auditors a clean, structured, machine-readable extract of the general ledger and sub-ledgers, which significantly streamlines their audit procedures.

In our customer engagements, we have seen companies use these reports primarily for business audit purposes — not because of a government nomination, but because their external auditor requested the data in this format as part of the annual audit engagement.

Any China legal entity that undergoes professional auditing should have this capability ready.

The Solution at a Glance

SAP delivers four XML report components under a single configuration framework:

Report Content

Common InformationEnterprise master data, currency, exchange rates, vendor/customer profiles
General Ledger (GL)Journal entries, account balances, cash flow items
AP/ARReceivables and payables statements
Fixed Assets (AA)Asset cards, changes, retirements

One thing worth noting upfront: all XML element names in the generated files are in Chinese, as defined by the GB/T 24589.01-2010 national standard. SAP's implementation preserves this exactly. When you open the output or troubleshoot an empty section, the element names you encounter will be Chinese — this is by design, not a localization gap. Part 2 of this series lists key elements with English labels to help you navigate.

The process is straightforward:

  1. Configure — Fiori app: Define Settings for Standardized Data Interface – China
  2. Generate — Fiori app: Run Statutory Reports
  3. Submit — Deliver XML files to the audit authority or external auditor

Official Documentation

For step-by-step configuration, the Help Portal is your primary reference:

  • Public Cloud: China Audit DRC Reports | Define Settings for Standardized Data Interface – China
  • Private Cloud / On-Premise: China Audit DRC Reports | Define Settings for Standardized Data Interface – China

Once you have read the official docs, head over to Part 2 of this series for the practical tips and gotchas that save you time during implementation.

Reference: SAP Note 3285703

entry.title: Migration from Crystal Reports 11.5 to Crystal Reports for Eclipse , Vulnerability Remediation
entry.content:
We are currently using SAP Crystal Reports Java Runtime 11.8.6.1371 (Crystal Reports 11.5/XI) in a Java application.

During a recent Veracode scan, the following vulnerabilities were reported in Crystal Reports runtime components:

  • CWE-377 – Insecure Temporary File
  • CWE-117 – Improper Output Neutralization for Logs (CRLF Injection)

The impacted libraries include:

  • webreporting.jar
  • CrystalExporters.jar
  • CrystalReportingCommon.jar
  • CRDBXmlServer.jar
  • CrystalCharting.jar

With the help of community forum got to know that Crystal Reports 11.5 is now End of Life and that no further patches are available. A previous response suggested migrating to Crystal Reports for Eclipse.

Before assessing this as a remediation option, I would appreciate guidance from anyone who has experience with this migration:

  1. Has anyone migrated from Crystal Reports 11.5/XI Java Runtime to Crystal Reports for Eclipse?
  2. Was the migration a straightforward upgrade, or did it require significant effort?
  3. Did it involve:
    • Application code changes?
    • SDK/API replacement?
    • Report redevelopment or modification?
    • Project refactoring and regression testing?
  4. Are there any migration guides, best practices, or lessons learned that you would recommend?
  5. Most importantly, did the migration help address or eliminate security findings reported against the legacy runtime, including:
    • CWE-377 (Insecure Temporary File)
    • CWE-117 (CRLF Injection / Improper Output Neutralization for Logs)
  6. Were the affected runtime libraries replaced as part of the migration, and did subsequent security scans no longer report these findings?

We are trying to determine whether migration to Crystal Reports for Eclipse is a viable remediation path for these vulnerabilities and whether it should be treated as a simple upgrade or as a larger modernization effort for planning purposes.

Thank you for any guidance or experience you can share.

entry.title: China Audit Reports (CADE) — Practical Tips and Gotchas
entry.content:
This is Part 2 of the CADE series. For business context and official documentation links, see [Part 1 — What It Is and Who Needs It].

CADE's Help Portal documentation covers the configuration steps well. This post focuses on what the docs don't make obvious — patterns from real customer cases that cost time when missed. Where Chinese XML element names appear, English labels are included for reference.

Gotcha 1: "Set to Completed" Is Not Optional

The most common first-run error. In the Define Settings for SDI – China app, you must click Review → Set to Completed after finishing all 7 configuration steps. Clicking Save leaves the configuration in draft status, which causes a hard error at runtime.

This applies equally to the CADE all reports: the "Reporting Company Value not found" error is almost always a sign the configuration status is not Completed.

Gotcha 2: Silent Exclusions — Unmapped = Not Exported

Two configuration steps follow the same pattern: if you don't map it, it's silently excluded from the XML. No warning, no error.

  • Step 2 (Exchange Rate Type Mapping): Only mapped exchange rate types appear in the Common Information XML. Unmapped types are excluded entirely.
  • Step 3 (Journal Entry Type Mapping): One entry per GB/T type category must be marked as the leading type. When a document type is not mapped, the system outputs the leading type instead — producing valid-looking XML that may misclassify transactions.

Map all exchange rate types and document types your company uses. Choose leading types deliberately.

Gotcha 3: Accounts Outside the FSV Are Excluded from GL Output

Context: Financial Statement Version (FSV) is the legacy hierarchy tool that CADE still relies on for Step 6 (G/L Account Details). Note that FSV has been superseded by Universal Hierarchy in S/4HANA — but CADE has not yet migrated to the new solution. This means your FSV must be maintained and up to date even if your broader setup has moved to Universal Hierarchy.

Only G/L accounts included in the FSV are extracted into the GL XML. Accounts that exist in the system but are not assigned to the FSV will not appear in the audit file — no warning, no error.

Hidden trick: You can override the account Type value in the XML by wrapping the desired type in # characters within the FSV node description — for example: #Four-Digit Account Code #Bank Deposit. The system reads what is between the # markers as the Type field value. Useful when the account group in master data does not match the GB/T classification required.

Gotcha 4: Subsidiary Accounting Items — Four Come Free, More Need Configuration

Step 5 has four system SAIs always active: Supplier (LIFNR), Customer (KUNNR), Cost Center (RCNTR), Functional Area (RFAREA). No setup needed for these.

If the XML element 科目辅助核算 (Account Auxiliary Accounting) shows 0 occurrences, the issue is almost always that custom SAIs were not configured. Go to the Subsidiary Accounting Items tab and assign the dimensions you need from the 23 available ACDOCA fields.

Gotcha 5: Cash Flow — One Valid Approach in Public Cloud

Step 1 (General Information) has a Liquidity Item Hierarchy field. For the cash flow statement, select "Not Used for Cash Flow Report" and configure the cash flow report structure in Step 7 (Statutory Reporting) instead.

The alternative option ("Used for Cash Flow Report", based on Liquidity Item Hierarchy from Advanced Cash Management) has been sunset for China. The offsetting accounts solution is the only supported approach. All Public Cloud customers are on this path by default.

If 现金流量项目 (Cash Flow Items) shows 0 occurrences:

  1. Confirm Step 1 is set to "Not Used for Cash Flow Report"
  2. Confirm Step 7 has the cash flow report structure assigned
  3. Check whether Online Offsetting Account Determination was activated — only documents posted after activation are included. For earlier documents, a migration step is required (Public Cloud migration guide).

Gotcha 6: Fixed Assets — Three Common Empty Sections

The AA report is the module most prone to silent empty sections:

XML Element English Label Root Cause Fix

固定资产卡片Asset CardIncorrect or missing fiscal year variantCheck fiscal year variant assignment on company code
固定资产变动情况Asset ChangesNo change documents in periodSourced from Change Documents — empty by design if no movements were posted
固定资产处置情况Asset RetirementsWrong AssetTransactionVariantMust be 2 (Retirement) when posting via ABAVN or ABAON; transaction type 210 = prior-year, 260 = current-year

Gotcha 7: Step 7 (Statutory Reports) Is Optional for GL — But You'll Get a Warning

In the Define Settings for SDI – China app, Step 7 (Statutory Reporting) is where you assign the financial statement structures (balance sheet, income statement, etc.) that are embedded in the GL audit file.

What's not obvious: if you skip Step 7 entirely, the CN_GBT24589_GL report will still generate successfully — but with a warning message indicating that the statutory reports section is empty. The journal entry and account balance data will be there; only the financial statement part will be missing.

This means Step 7 is optional depending on what the auditor actually needs. If they only require the GL transaction data and not the embedded financial statements, you can defer this configuration. But if the auditor expects complete output, Step 7 must be configured before submission.

Still Stuck?

Open a customer incident with:

  • System release and SP level
  • Exact XML element name showing 0 occurrences or exact error message
  • Screenshot of Define Settings for Standardized Data Interface – China configuration
  • Sample FI document or asset master data for reproduction

Based on SAP standard GB/T 24589.01-2010 | Reference: SAP Note 3285703

entry.title: Decoding SAP BW Hierarchy Flat File Loading: Understanding Each Segment of a Hierarchy DataSource
entry.content:
Introduction

It so happened that while I was exploring time-dependent hierarchies in SAP BW, I encountered several challenges related to loading hierarchy data from CSV flat files into the different segments of a Hierarchy DataSource.

At first, I assumed hierarchy loading was similar to loading master data or transactional data—prepare a CSV, map the fields, execute the InfoPackage, and the hierarchy should be available. However, after multiple failed attempts and a considerable amount of investigation, I realized that hierarchy loading follows a different internal architecture.

Instead of relying solely on SAP documentation, I decided to reverse engineer the generated DataSource itself. By examining its segments, previewing the extracted data, inspecting the file structure, and analyzing the example file generated by SAP, I gradually understood how hierarchy data is interpreted internally.

This blog summarizes that learning journey and highlights the insights that helped me understand SAP BW hierarchy loading more deeply.

My objective was to create a time-dependent Transportation Network Hierarchy with the following structure:

As evident from this final snapshot, my aim was to incorporate :

  • Hierarchy-enabled InfoObjects
  • Time-dependent hierarchy
  • Flat File Hierarchy DataSource
  • Hierarchy loading using InfoPackage and DTP

Building a Transportation Network Hierarchy

I created four hierarchy-enabled InfoObjects.

Level InfoObject

CountryZCUNTRI6
RegionZREGN6
PlantZPLAANT6
WarehouseZWRHUS6

The hierarchy structure was prepared using a CSV file containing:

  • NODEID
  • Parent ID
  • InfoObject Name
  • Node Name
  • Valid From
  • Valid To
NODEID,IOBJNM,NODENAME,LINK,PARENTID,DATEFROM,DATETO
1,0HIER_NODE,WORLD,,0,20240101,99991231
2,ZCUNTRI6,INDIA,,1,20240101,99991231
3,ZREGN6,NORTH_REGION,,2,20240101,99991231
4,ZPLAANT6,DELHI_PLANT,,3,20240101,99991231
5,ZWRHUS6,DELHI_WH1,,4,20240101,20241231
6,ZREGN6,SOUTH_REGION,,2,20240101,99991231
7,ZPLAANT6,CHENNAI_PLANT,,6,20240101,99991231
8,ZWRHUS6,CHENNAI_WH,,7,20240101,99991231
9,ZCUNTRI6,UNITED_STATES,,1,20240101,99991231
10,ZREGN6,EAST_REGION,,9,20240101,99991231
11,ZPLAANT6,NEWYORK_PLANT,,10,20240101,99991231
12,ZWRHUS6,NEWYORK_WH,,11,20240101,99991231
13,ZREGN6,WEST_REGION,,9,20240101,99991231
14,ZPLAANT6,CALIFORNIA_PLANT,,13,20240101,99991231
15,ZWRHUS6,CALIFORNIA_WH,,14,20240101,99991231

Configuring the Flat File Hierarchy DataSource

The next step was creating a Flat File Hierarchy DataSource.

The DataSource was configured using the Hierarchy Separated by Separator converter and linked to the target hierarchy.

Initially, everything appeared straightforward as I linked a single CSV file for my Hierarchy by browsing the device storage.

The First Surprise – Multiple Hierarchy Segments

When I opened the preview of the generated DataSource, I noticed something unexpected.

Instead of having a single hierarchy structure, SAP had created multiple logical segments.

These included:

  • Hierarchy Header
  • Hierarchy Description
  • Hierarchy Structure
  • Description for Text

Initially, I did not understand why SAP separated the hierarchy into multiple segments.

Only after exploring each segment individually did their purpose become much clearer.

Understanding the Purpose of Each Segment

1. Hierarchy Header

  • The Hierarchy Header segment acts as the identification and control record for the entire hierarchy being loaded into SAP BW. It contains information that uniquely identifies the hierarchy and its version before any node relationships or texts are processed.
  • The field HIENM (Hierarchy Name) specifies the name of the hierarchy, such as Transportation Network Hierarchy, while HIER_VERS (Hierarchy Version) identifies the particular version of that hierarchy.
  • This is important because organizations often maintain multiple hierarchy versions for different business periods, planning scenarios, or reporting requirements. During hierarchy loading, BW first reads the header segment to determine which hierarchy should be created or updated. Without this segment, BW would not know where the incoming hierarchy structure belongs.
HIENM      = TRANSPORT_NETWORK
HIER_VERS  = 001

2. Hierarchy Description

  • The Hierarchy Description segment stores the descriptive texts of the hierarchy itself.
  • While the header identifies the hierarchy technically, the description segment provides business-friendly names that users can see in reporting tools, BEx queries, Analysis for Office, and BW hierarchy selections.
  • The field LANGU specifies the language, allowing the same hierarchy to have multilingual descriptions. The fields TXTSH, TXTMD, and TXTLG store short, medium, and long descriptions respectively.
English: Transportation Network Hierarchy
German : Transportnetz Hierarchie

 

3. Hierarchy Structure

  • Initially I assumed this was the only segment that mattered. However, after examining the preview, I realized that SAP was building the hierarchy using this segment while the remaining segments supplied metadata and texts.
  • Fields such as NODEID, PARENTID, and NODENAME establish the hierarchical organization of business entities. For example, a Country node can be linked to a Region node, which in turn can be linked to a Plant and subsequently a Warehouse.
  • BW uses NODEID as a unique technical identifier for each node and PARENTID to determine the node's superior level in the hierarchy.
  • The fields DATEFROM and DATETO enable time-dependent hierarchies by defining the period during which a node relationship is valid.
  • This allows organizational structures to change over time while preserving historical reporting accuracy. During loading, BW reads these records to construct the hierarchy tree exactly as defined in the source file.
COUNTRY
└─ REGION
     └─ PLANT
          └─ WAREHOUSE

4. Description for Text

  • The Node Description segment provides multilingual texts for individual hierarchy nodes. Unlike the Hierarchy Description segment, which describes the hierarchy itself, this segment describes every node within the hierarchy.
  • The field NODENAME identifies the node, while LANGU, TXTSH, TXTMD, and TXTLG store the corresponding short, medium, and long texts in different languages.
Node: INDIA
English: India
German : Indien

Node: EAST
English: Eastern Region
German : Ost Region

 

Difference Between Segment 2 and Segment 4

Although both segments contain language-dependent texts, they serve completely different purposes.

Segment 2 (Hierarchy Description) describes the entire hierarchy as a single object. It provides the name that users see when selecting or viewing the hierarchy while Segment 4 (Node Descriptions) describes the individual nodes inside the hierarchy.

Using Preview as a Debugging Tool

One of the most useful discoveries during this exercise was the Preview functionality.

Instead of immediately executing an InfoPackage, previewing the DataSource allowed me to verify:

  • Whether the hierarchy records were correctly interpreted.
  • Whether each segment contained the expected data.
  • Whether the CSV file matched the DataSource definition.

This proved invaluable while debugging hierarchy loads as I discovered that all these four segments of the Hierarchy Datasource have to be referenced from the single flat-file CSV I supplied.

Understanding the File Structure

To better understand how SAP expected the flat file to be organized, I opened the File Structure definition.

This displayed every field expected by the hierarchy converter, including: NODEID, IOBJNM, NODENAME, LINK, PARENTID, DATETO, DATEFROM, LANGU, TXTSH, TXTMD and TXTLG

Initially, seeing all these fields together suggested that every hierarchy record should contain all of them. However, further investigation revealed that the converter processes these fields according to the logical hierarchy segments rather than treating them as a single, uniform record layout.

This observation significantly improved my understanding of how hierarchy flat files are interpreted.

The Hidden Gem – "Create Example File"

One feature that completely changed my approach was the Create Example File button.

Rather than guessing the expected file format, SAP generated a sample hierarchy file demonstrating the required structure.

Studying this example clarified several assumptions I had made during development and provided a reliable reference for validating my own hierarchy file.

In hindsight, this was probably the most valuable debugging tool available for hierarchy flat files.

 

The Example File comprised the following template csv structure for the Flat file to be fed to the BW Datasource :

1,0HIER_NODE,ROOT,,0,29991231,20260709,E,Root,Root Node,Root of Hierarchy
2,0HIER_NODE,SUB, ,1,29991231,20260709,E,Under,Subnode,Subnode of Hierarchy
3,ZWRHUS6,LEAF,,2,29991231,20260709,,,,
4,ZWRHUS6,2NDLEAF,,1,29991231,20260709,,,,
5,0HIER_NODE,SUB,X,4,29991231,20260709,E,Under,Subnode,Subnode of Hierarchy

Time Dependency

One of my secondary requirements was implementing a time-dependent hierarchy.

Each hierarchy node contains validity dates.

For example:

Warehouse Valid From Valid To

DELHI_WH12024010120241231

This allows SAP BW to display hierarchy structures according to the reporting period, making the hierarchy dynamic over time rather than static.

Lessons Learned

This assignment taught me much more than simply creating a hierarchy.

Some of the key takeaways were:

  • A hierarchy DataSource is internally divided into multiple logical segments.
  • The hierarchy structure is only one part of the complete hierarchy definition.
  • Previewing each segment before loading can save considerable debugging time.
  • The generated File Structure reveals how SAP expects hierarchy data to be interpreted.
  • The Create Example File feature is an excellent way to understand the expected flat-file format.
  • Reverse engineering SAP-generated metadata often provides deeper understanding than simply following step-by-step instructions.

Common Mistakes When Loading Hierarchies

  • Mistake 1: Assuming only the Hierarchy Structure segment matters.
  • Mistake 2: Ignoring the generated example file.
  • Mistake 3: Not checking Preview before InfoPackage execution.
  • Mistake 4: Incorrect DATEFROM/DATETO values.

Conclusion

At last to conclude, although this started as a simple exploration journey on time-dependent hierarchies, it eventually became an exercise in understanding how SAP BW internally models hierarchy data.

Rather than focusing solely on making the hierarchy load successfully, examining the generated DataSource, understanding the role of each hierarchy segment, and exploring SAP's built-in debugging features helped me gain a much deeper appreciation of the hierarchy framework.

I hope these observations prove useful to anyone beginning their journey with SAP BW hierarchies or trying to understand what happens behind the scenes during hierarchy loading. Thank You.

entry.title: Foundational Success Plan news: July updates
entry.content:
Welcome to the first edition of our Foundational Success Plan monthly blog—your go-to source for the latest updates, insights, and opportunities designed to help you get the most from your SAP cloud solutions.

Included with every SAP cloud solution and built on SAP’s trusted Enterprise Support foundation, the Foundational Success Plan helps you accelerate adoption, strengthen business continuity, and maximize the value of your SAP investments.

Each month, we’ll share the latest updates, expert insights, new offerings, customer programs, and events to help you stay informed, embrace innovation, and achieve your business goals.

In this July edition, discover key highlights and resources designed to support your success and help you get even more value from SAP.

Explore the latest enhancements and see how a modernized interface boosts your SAP experience.

In the latest ASUGTalks episode, Stefan Steinle, EVP and Head of Global Customer Support at SAP, discusses how customer support is evolving from reactive issue resolution to proactive, AI-enabled engagement that helps organizations accelerate transformation and innovation.

He explores SAP’s three support pillars—Invisible Support, Empowered Support, and Accelerated Support—and shares how agentic AI is enhancing migration and transformation projects while keeping the human element at the heart of the customer experience.

entry.title: Re: STO BRASIL - Determinação direito fiscal ICMS
entry.content:
Boa noite 

Ainda possui essa pendência?

Vou tentar opinar com base nas suas imagens, reparei que na imagem o Código de Imposto SD atribuído na remessa é I0.

Execute este teste: Copie o registro do grupo 15 e troque o YS para I0.

Só será permitido informar o Código I0 como chave, se também existir um código I0 criado na visão de Código de Imposto MM.

Utilizei essa estratégia para demonstrar para o usuário que o Direito Fiscal informado nos grupos relacionados a Venda também determinavam o Direito Fiscal nas Transferências entre Centros.

Apesar da Remessa identificar o Código de Imposto SD nas chaves dinâmicas e atribuir o Direito Fiscal,  Alíquota e Base deste registro não serão considerados nos cálculos da transferência , visto que o cálculo estará orientado para o Código YE e YS.

Caso ajude, não deixe de retornar aqui e passar as suas considerações.

entry.title: The paging catastrophe
entry.content:
• Introduction
  • Context
  • Case 1: No BTP pre-filtering or post-filtering
  • Case 2: BTP post-filtering
  • Case 3: BTP pre-filtering
  • Case 4: Split BTP pre-filtering
  • Conclusions

Introduction

In the late 19th century, the scientific community started to face unexpected issues, some of them quite serious, upon proven experiments for which no accepted formula was able to produce reasonable prediction or explanation. This originated the entire collapse of the current physics, now called ‘classic physics’, into a new frame ignited by the quantum mechanics. The game-changing problem was named ‘the ultraviolet catastrophe’.

Similarly to what happened back then –hopefully less ontologically problematic–, the SAP transition from classic to Fiori-oriented development brings about some anomalies not especially easy to overcome without a systemic revolution of the technology itself. One of the problems we have experienced regards the paging mechanism when rendering a Fiori list report via back-end OData service.

Context

Let’s assume the following scenario:

  1. On-premise OData service.

  2. BTP Fiori list report application consuming the back-end service via custom entity.

    1. Respecting the Clean Core principles, the report combines information from the back-end –only read–, and local BTP tables. To ensure integrity, the key fields are the same on both sides.

    2. Consequently, the selection filters can refer to both back-end and BTP fields.

  3. The BTP application, fields and tables underneath dominate the entire execution engine. This is to say, BTP-located filtering, if any, must lead the back-end selection.

Case 1: No BTP pre-filtering or post-filtering

Scenario

No BTP-located filter fields are set, so there is no need to implement BTP filtering before or after the service call. In real executions, this is not very likely to happen, but anyway interesting to start exposing the problem.

Behaviour

All pages are correctly delivered through scrolling. Considering n items are hit in the back-end, when reaching the last page, all of them are rendered.

Case 2: BTP post-filtering

Scenario

Here, BTP-located filter fields are set. The idea is to retrieve matching information from the back-end and, next, filter by the BTP conditions.

Behaviour

If the page is not reduced during the post filtering, i.e., no line is impacted by the BTP filtering, then it is correctly shown and the scroll for the next one continues to be enabled.

Else, if the page size is reduced, the system considers it is the last so no more scrolling is enabled; even trying to scroll down no more service calls are run. The page cropping leads then to missing information.

Case 3: BTP pre-filtering

Scenario

If the post-filtering does not work, why not first filter in BTP and then use the key fields to retrieve from the back-end only exactly what is needed?

Behaviour

Here, x items are pre-filtered in BTP. If, additionally, back-end filtering is set, the final number of retrieved items, say n, can potentially be less than x.

Anyway, the strategy seems finely focused but the problem arises when too many items are pre-filtered so the URL to call the service is too long to be accepted and leads to an exception.

Case 4: Split BTP pre-filtering

Scenario

Considering some trust was put in the previous step, maybe we could split the filtering and manually call the service in small packages to avoid problems with the URL.

Behaviour

To avoid the cropped page problem, the main purpose is to always build complete pages to keep the execution alive; otherwise, it stops. The challenge is to seamlessly advance through packages as if no additional calls were executed.

In BTP, x items are filtered and supposed to be sent to the back-end. The URL length is first checked:

  • If it is short enough to be accepted, then no split is needed and the call can be executed as conventionally.

  • In other case, the logic will split the x items into small packages of s; the package size must be greater than the page size so as to avoid the scroll disabling.

For the first call, the s items hit n back-end items, so the report counter shows n as the total number of items, but this value is not correct, as there are more items waiting to be sent.

By scrolling down, the complete list of n items related to the first package is delivered correctly, so for now no need to process other packages.

If the current page size is less than the determined size, i.e., if the page is cropped, this means we have reached the end of the package, so the next one can be processed.

The next package is then processed making sure the skip is reset to retrieve all back-end hits from the beginning. The cropped page is then completed with p - q items from the new package.

For next page the skip is automatically set to the page size and because there is no option to keep track of the actual index we are in –stateless processing–, a gap of missing information can occur.

Conclusions

The development of BTP reports via custom entities where local filtering is supposed to restrain the information coming from the back-end introduces a series of problems not easy to overcome. Business may claim classic development where all the data and logic was set on-premise did not carry this type of problems.

As it happened to physics, the transition to modern SAP development presents by comparison some obstacles for which quick adaptation is needed. The answer to this problem, something like quantum mechanics in our recurrent reference, goes through using a more powerful technology; for the time being, external entities based on HANA virtual tables are the best alternative to avoid the paging catastrophe.

entry.title: Parallel Processing in ABAP using SPTA Framework
entry.content:
Introduction:

During my SAP ABAP development experience, I have worked on reports and interfaces that process large volumes of business data. One challenge I frequently noticed is that even well-written ABAP programs can become slow when they process thousands of records sequentially. As data volume grows, runtime increases significantly, affecting overall system performance and the user experience.

While exploring different performance optimization techniques, I came across the SPTA (SAP Parallel Task Administration) framework. What interested me most was that SAP already provides a standard mechanism to execute workloads in parallel instead of requiring developers to build a custom parallel processing solution from scratch. The framework takes care of distributing work packets, managing parallel tasks, utilizing available work processes, and collecting the results after processing is complete.

To better understand how SPTA works internally, I built a simple prototype instead of directly using a business scenario. The example generates random data, splits it into multiple work packets, processes each packet in parallel, and finally combines the results. This hands-on exercise helped me understand the complete flow of parallel processing and how the framework manages task execution behind the scenes.

In this blog, I will walk through the same example step by step, explain the key components involved, and share the learnings I gained while implementing the SPTA framework. I hope this practical approach helps anyone who is getting started with parallel processing in SAP ABAP.

What is SPTA Framework?

SPTA is a standard SAP framework designed to execute independent units of work in parallel. It internally manages: 

  • Allocation of available work processes 
  • Load balancing across application servers 
  • Asynchronous RFC execution 
  • Work packet management 
  • Error handling and task synchronization 
  • Collection of results after task completion 

Instead of manually creating asynchronous RFC tasks using STARTING NEW TASK, developers can leverage the SPTA framework to simplify implementation and improve maintainability.

Solution Overview- 

The program follows the below execution flow:

The framework repeatedly executes these callback routines until all records are processed.

TYPE-POOLS: spta.
TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_final,
        number TYPE char3,
        index TYPE char3,
       END OF ty_final.

TYPES: BEGIN OF ztest_random_data,
         number TYPE char3,
         index  TYPE char3,
       END OF ztest_random_data.

TYPES ztest_random_data_t TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ztest_random_data
                          WITH EMPTY KEY.
DATA:
      it_random TYPE ztest_random_data_t,
      it_final TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF ty_final,
      v_max_no_of_tasks TYPE syindex.

* Create random numbers
PERFORM f_create_random_no.
* Write random numbers before calling parallel processing
PERFORM f_write_random_rec.

 "Fetch the server group
  SELECT SINGLE classname
  INTO @DATA(lv_server_grp)
  FROM rzllitab
  WHERE grouptype = 'S'.

  IF sy-subrc NE 0.
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

* Calling the function module to invoke parallel processing
* framwork

CALL FUNCTION 'SPTA_PARA_PROCESS_START_2'
  EXPORTING
    server_group             = lv_server_grp
    before_rfc_callback_form = 'BEFORE_RFC'
    in_rfc_callback_form     = 'IN_RFC'
    after_rfc_callback_form  = 'AFTER_RFC'
    callback_prog            = 'ZTEST_SPTA_FRAME_WORK'
  CHANGING
    user_param               = it_random
  EXCEPTIONS
    invalid_server_group     = 1
    no_resources_available   = 2
    OTHERS                   = 3.

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  BEFORE_RFC
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* In this routine work packets are prepared for parallel processing   *
* also check the variable for downstream processing                    *
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM before_rfc
    USING    is_before_rfc_imp     TYPE spta_t_before_rfc_imp
    CHANGING es_before_rfc_exp     TYPE spta_t_before_rfc_exp
             it_rfcdata            TYPE spta_t_indxtab
             ct_failed_objects     TYPE spta_t_failed_objects
             et_objects_in_process TYPE spta_t_objects_in_process
             it_data               TYPE ztest_random_data_t.

  DATA: l_counter     TYPE i,
        l_no_of_items TYPE i,
        it_final TYPE ztest_random_data_t,
        wa_data TYPE ztest_random_data.

* Create small work packets for parallel processing
* This is custom logic
  l_no_of_items = 10.    " One work packet will contain 10 records

  LOOP AT it_data INTO wa_data.
* Read and move specified number of records
    IF ( l_counter < l_no_of_items
      AND wa_data IS NOT INITIAL ).
      APPEND wa_data TO it_final.
      DELETE it_data INDEX 1.
      l_counter = l_counter + 1.
    ELSE.
      EXIT.
    ENDIF.
  ENDLOOP.

* Convert the input data into the INDX structure that is needed for the RFC
  CALL FUNCTION 'SPTA_INDX_PACKAGE_ENCODE'
    EXPORTING
      data    = it_final
    IMPORTING
      indxtab = it_rfcdata.

* This is mandatory step
* inform task manager that an rfc can be started from the data compiled
  IF it_final IS NOT INITIAL.
    es_before_rfc_exp-start_rfc = 'X'.
  ELSE.
    CLEAR es_before_rfc_exp-start_rfc.
    RETURN.
  ENDIF.

ENDFORM.                    " BEFORE_RFC_CALLBACK_FORM
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  IN_RFC
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*  In this routine create own RFC enabled FM or write logic in this    *
*  routine                          *
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM in_rfc
         USING is_in_rfc_imp         TYPE spta_t_in_rfc_imp "#EC NEEDED
         CHANGING es_in_rfc_exp      TYPE spta_t_in_rfc_exp
                  it_rfcdata         TYPE spta_t_indxtab.

  DATA: it_final TYPE ztest_random_data_t.

* Decode the data from the INDX Structure into the process work list
  CALL FUNCTION 'SPTA_INDX_PACKAGE_DECODE'
    EXPORTING
      indxtab = it_rfcdata
    IMPORTING
      data    = it_final.

* Simple logic to sort data
  SORT it_final BY number.

* Again Encode output data for AFTER_RFC form
  CALL FUNCTION 'SPTA_INDX_PACKAGE_ENCODE'
    EXPORTING
      data    = it_final
    IMPORTING
      indxtab = it_rfcdata.

ENDFORM.                    "in_rfc
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  AFTER_RFC
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
* In this routine we have to collect date after parallel processing    *
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM after_rfc
     USING it_rfcdata            TYPE spta_t_indxtab
           if_rfcsubrc           TYPE sy-subrc
           if_rfcmsg             TYPE spta_t_rfcmsg
           it_objects_in_process TYPE spta_t_objects_in_process "#EC NEEDED
           is_after_rfc_imp      TYPE spta_t_after_rfc_imp  "#EC NEEDED
  CHANGING es_after_rfc_exp      TYPE spta_t_after_rfc_exp  "#EC NEEDED
           cs_user_param         TYPE ztest_random_data_t.

  DATA: wa_final TYPE ztest_random_data.

* Decode RFC output data and add RFC-results to global data
  CALL FUNCTION 'SPTA_INDX_PACKAGE_DECODE'
    EXPORTING
      indxtab = it_rfcdata
    IMPORTING
      data    = it_final.

* After parallel processing write all the records
  LOOP AT it_final INTO wa_final.
    WRITE:/ wa_final-number, 10 wa_final-index.
    CLEAR wa_final.
  ENDLOOP.
  WRITE: sy-uline.

ENDFORM.                    "after_rfc
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  F_CREATE_RANDOM_NO
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
*  -->  p1        text
*  <--  p2        text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM f_create_random_no.

  DATA: lcl_random TYPE REF TO cl_abap_random_int,
        l_index TYPE sytabix,
        lwa_data TYPE ztest_random_data,
        l_min TYPE i,
        l_max TYPE i.
  l_min = 10.
  l_max = 90.
  TRY.
      lcl_random = cl_abap_random_int=>create( min = l_min
                                               max = l_max ).
    CATCH cx_abap_random.
  ENDTRY.

  DO 100 TIMES.
*   Get the Random Number:
    lwa_data-number = lcl_random->get_next( ).
    l_index = l_index + 1.
    lwa_data-index = l_index.
    APPEND lwa_data TO it_random.
  ENDDO.

ENDFORM.                    " F_CREATE_RANDOM_NO
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*&      Form  F_WRITE_RANDOM_REC
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*
*       text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
*  -->  p1        text
*  <--  p2        text
*----------------------------------------------------------------------*
FORM f_write_random_rec.

  DATA: wa_final TYPE ztest_random_data.
  WRITE:/ 'Before parallel processing start'.
  LOOP AT it_random INTO wa_final.
    WRITE:/ wa_final-number, 10 wa_final-index.
    CLEAR wa_final.
  ENDLOOP.
  WRITE:/ 'After end parallel processing'.

ENDFORM.                    " F_WRITE_RANDOM_REC*&---------------------------------------------------------------------*

Generate Sample Data-

FORM f_create_random_no. 
 
  DATA: lcl_random TYPE REF TO cl_abap_random_int, 
        l_index TYPE sytabix, 
        lwa_data TYPE ztest_random_data, 
        l_min TYPE i, 
        l_max TYPE i. 
  l_min = 10. 
  l_max = 90. 
 
  TRY. 
      lcl_random = cl_abap_random_int=>create( min = l_min 
                                               max = l_max ). 
    CATCH cx_abap_random. 
  ENDTRY. 
 
  DO 100 TIMES. 
*   Get the Random Number: 
    lwa_data-number = lcl_random->get_next( ). 
    l_index = l_index + 1. 
    lwa_data-index = l_index. 
    APPEND lwa_data TO it_random. 
  ENDDO. 
 
ENDFORM.                    " F_CREATE_RANDOM_NO

This routine generates 100 random records and stores them in IT_RANDOM. 

Example: 

NUMBER   INDEX 
------------------------
45                  1 
72                  2 
18                 3 
63                 4

At this point, all records are available for processing.

Determine Server Group- 

 "Fetch the server group 
  SELECT SINGLE classname 
  INTO @DATA(lv_server_grp) 
  FROM rzllitab 
  WHERE grouptype = 'S'.

  IF sy-subrc NE 0. 
    RETURN. 
  ENDIF.

The SPTA framework requires an RFC Server Group. 

Server groups are configured in transaction: RZ12 

The framework uses this server group to determine where parallel tasks can run.

Start the SPTA Framework-

* Calling the function module to invoke parallel processing 
* framwork 
CALL FUNCTION 'SPTA_PARA_PROCESS_START_2' 
  EXPORTING 
    server_group             = lv_server_grp 
    before_rfc_callback_form = 'BEFORE_RFC' 
    in_rfc_callback_form     = 'IN_RFC' 
    after_rfc_callback_form  = 'AFTER_RFC' 
    callback_prog            = 'ZTEST_SPTA_FRAME_WORK' 
  CHANGING 
    user_param               = it_random 
  EXCEPTIONS 
    invalid_server_group     = 1 
    no_resources_available   = 2 
    OTHERS                   = 3.

This is the entry point of the framework. 

The entire table IT_RANDOM is passed through: 

user_param = it_random 

Internally, SPTA passes this table to BEFORE_RFC, where work packets are created. 

The framework then repeatedly executes: 

  1. BEFORE_RFC 
  1. IN_RFC 
  1. AFTER_RFC 

Until all records have been processed.

 BEFORE_RFC: Create Work Packets-

This callback prepares the data packets that will be processed in parallel.

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------* 
*&      Form  BEFORE_RFC 
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------* 
* In this routine work packets are prepared for parallel processing   * 
* also check the variable for downstream processing                    * 
*----------------------------------------------------------------------* 
FORM before_rfc 
    USING    is_before_rfc_imp     TYPE spta_t_before_rfc_imp 
    CHANGING es_before_rfc_exp     TYPE spta_t_before_rfc_exp 
             it_rfcdata            TYPE spta_t_indxtab 
             ct_failed_objects     TYPE spta_t_failed_objects 
             et_objects_in_process TYPE spta_t_objects_in_process
             it_data               TYPE ztest_random_data_t. 
 
  DATA: l_counter     TYPE i, 
        l_no_of_items TYPE i, 
        it_final TYPE ztest_random_data_t, 
        wa_data TYPE ztest_random_data. 
 
* Create small work packets for parallel processing 
* This is custom logic 
  l_no_of_items = 10.    " One work packet will contain 10 records 
  LOOP AT it_data INTO wa_data. 
* Read and move specified number of records 
    IF ( l_counter < l_no_of_items 
      AND wa_data IS NOT INITIAL ). 
      APPEND wa_data TO it_final. 
      DELETE it_data INDEX 1. 
      l_counter = l_counter + 1. 
    ELSE. 
      EXIT. 
    ENDIF. 
  ENDLOOP. 
 
* Convert the input data into the INDX structure that is needed for the RFC 
  CALL FUNCTION 'SPTA_INDX_PACKAGE_ENCODE' 
    EXPORTING 
      data    = it_final 
    IMPORTING 
      indxtab = it_rfcdata. 
 
* This is mandatory step 
* inform task manager that an rfc can be started from the data compiled 
  IF it_final IS NOT INITIAL. 
    es_before_rfc_exp-start_rfc = 'X'. 
  ELSE. 
    CLEAR es_before_rfc_exp-start_rfc. 
    RETURN. 
  ENDIF. 
 
ENDFORM.                    " BEFORE_RFC_CALLBACK_FORM

Each work packet contains 10 records. 

For 100 records: 

Packet 1 -> Records 1 to 10 
Packet 2 -> Records 11 to 20 
Packet 3 -> Records 21 to 30 
... 
Packet 10 -> Records 91 to 100

The framework passes the remaining data in IT_DATA. 

This logic: 

  • Copies 10 records into IT_FINAL 
  • Removes them from IT_DATA 
  • Leaves the remaining records for the next callback execution 

Example: 

Before: 

IT_DATA = 100 records 

After first packet: 

IT_FINAL = 10 records 
IT_DATA  = 90 records

Convert Packet into RFC Format-

SPTA transfers data between work processes using INDX structures. 

This function serializes the packet into a format suitable for RFC communication.

Signal Framework to Start Processing-

This informs the framework that a work packet is ready, and a new RFC task can be started.

IN_RFC: Execute Business Logic- 

This routine runs in a separate work process.

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------* 
*&      Form  IN_RFC 
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------* 
*  In this routine create own RFC enabled FM or write logic in this    * 
*  routine                          * 
*----------------------------------------------------------------------* 
FORM in_rfc 
         USING is_in_rfc_imp         TYPE spta_t_in_rfc_imp "#EC NEEDED 
         CHANGING es_in_rfc_exp      TYPE spta_t_in_rfc_exp
                  it_rfcdata TYPE spta_t_indxtab. 

  DATA: it_final TYPE ztest_random_data_t. 
 
* Decode the data from the INDX Structure into the process work list 
  CALL FUNCTION 'SPTA_INDX_PACKAGE_DECODE' 
    EXPORTING 
      indxtab = it_rfcdata 
    IMPORTING 
      data    = it_final. 
 
* Simple logic to sort data 
  SORT it_final BY number. 
 
* Again Encode output data for AFTER_RFC form 
  CALL FUNCTION 'SPTA_INDX_PACKAGE_ENCODE' 
    EXPORTING 
      data    = it_final 
    IMPORTING 
      indxtab = it_rfcdata. 
 
ENDFORM.                    "in_rfc

Decode Packet-

The encoded packet is converted back into an internal table -> process data -> then packet is sorted by the random number. 

Encode Results-

The processed data is encoded again so that it can be returned to the caller. 

AFTER_RFC: Collect Results- 

Once a parallel task is complete, the framework invokes AFTER_RFC.

*&---------------------------------------------------------------------* 
*&      Form  AFTER_RFC 
*&---------------------------------------------------------------------* 
* In this routine we have to collect date after parallel processing    * 
*----------------------------------------------------------------------* 
FORM after_rfc 
     USING it_rfcdata            TYPE spta_t_indxtab 
           if_rfcsubrc           TYPE sy-subrc 
           if_rfcmsg             TYPE spta_t_rfcmsg 
           it_objects_in_process TYPE spta_t_objects_in_process "#EC NEEDED 
           is_after_rfc_imp      TYPE spta_t_after_rfc_imp  "#EC NEEDED 
  CHANGING es_after_rfc_exp      TYPE spta_t_after_rfc_exp  "#EC NEEDED 
           cs_user_param         TYPE ztest_random_data_t. 
 
  DATA: wa_final TYPE ztest_random_data. 
 
* Decode RFC output data and add RFC-results to global data 
  CALL FUNCTION 'SPTA_INDX_PACKAGE_DECODE' 
    EXPORTING 
      indxtab = it_rfcdata 
    IMPORTING 
      data    = it_final.
 
* After parallel processing write all the records 
  LOOP AT it_final INTO wa_final. 
    WRITE:/ wa_final-number, 10 wa_final-index. 
    CLEAR wa_final. 
  ENDLOOP. 
  WRITE: sy-uline. 
 
ENDFORM.                    "after_rfc

Decode Returned Data-

The processed packet is reconstructed -> The processed records are displayed.

Example-

Suppose the program generates:

45    1  
81    2  
23    3  
67    4  
... 

The framework creates:

Packet 1 -> 10 Records 
Packet 2 -> 10 Records 
Packet 3 -> 10 Records 
... 
Packet10 -> 10 Records

These packets are distributed among available work processes:

WP1 -> Packet 1 
WP2 -> Packet 2 
WP3 -> Packet 3 
WP4 -> Packet 4 

Each work process executes the logic inside IN_RFC independently and returns the results through AFTER_RFC.

Let’s execute and see--

Advantages of SPTA Framework- 

  • Standard SAP framework for parallel processing 
  • Automatic workload distribution 
  • Better utilization of available work processes 
  • Reduced execution time 
  • Simplified task management 
  • Improved scalability 
  • Centralized result collection 

Conclusion- 

The SPTA framework provides a robust and SAP-standard approach for implementing parallel processing in ABAP. By splitting large datasets into smaller work packets and distributing them across multiple work processes, developers can significantly improve application performance while keeping the implementation manageable. 

In this example, we generated random data, divided it into packets of 10 records, processed those packets in parallel, and collected the results using the three core callback routines: 

  • BEFORE_RFC – Creates work packets 
  • IN_RFC – Executes business logic in parallel 
  • AFTER_RFC – Collects and consolidates results 

For real-world scenarios involving mass data processing, SPTA offers a scalable and efficient solution that aligns with SAP development best practices.

entry.title: Batch Management in SAP LGM
entry.content:
Batch Management is used to track and manage materials in batches (lots) that share the same characteristics, such as Shelf life expiration date, Batch status, Country/region of origin, Date of manufacture etc.

This is needed to ensure Traceability, Quality Control, Shelf Life and Expiry Management, Inventory Differentiation, Warehouse and Production operations and also it helps during product recalls and are needed across a variety of Industries like Pharmaceuticals, Food & Beverage, Chemicals, Cosmetics, Medical Devices, Agriculture/Seeds and Manufacturing with lot-controlled materials.

Considering the importance of this feature SAP has introduced it in SAP LGM also. This blog covers an overview of how the functionality is designed as per current development.

We will cover it under following topics

  1. Batch Creation
  2. Stock Upload without Purchase Order
  3. Logistics Processes with Batch Management (Inbound & Outbound)
  4. Exception – How to handle scenario with missing Batch in S/4 document.

Lets start with the first topic

Batch Creation

  1. Batch Creation in S/4 system and transfer to LGM
    • Activate the Product as Batch Relevant and Define Batch in S/4 with key details like Date of Manufacture / SLED etc.

  • Within SAP LGM system using the App - Manage Replication Configuration, create a Replication variant that will be used for replicating batches from S/4 system to LGM

  • Using the App - Schedule Batch Replication configure the job to schedule the replication. This can be based on a schedule or even immediate

  • Once the Batches are replicated, they can be seen using the App - Display Batches

Let's now look at the Stock upload process without the Purchase Order

The Process starts with MIGO based GR posting in S/4 system. This creates Delivery in the S/4 system with batch details.

Based on the set up configurations a Logistical Demand is created and the batch details are available at the item level.

The corresponding Inbound Warehouse request also contains the batch details and once the warehousing process is completed the stock is updated back into S/4 system.

 

 

Let's now look at the Inbound and Outbound process:

Inbound Delivery based Batch Process

Once the Batch has been created in S/4 system and replicated in LGM, create the PO with the Batch Managed Product. The Batch details can either be provided in the PO or in Delivery.

Create Delivery for the PO with batch details.

On saving the Delivery, Logistical Demand is created in SAP LGM and the Batch Details are also seen in the document item.

 

 

The details also flow thru into the Inbound Warehouse request which can then be processed further to complete the warehouse processing.

 

Outbound process with Batch Management

The Outbound process starts with the creation of a Sales order and the Outbound Delivery within S/4 with a Batch managed product and Batch details.

Once the delivery is distributed it creates a Logistical Demand in SAP LGM. The Batch can be seen here at the item level.

In the above scenario this starts the warehouse processes and the Batch details flow into the Outbound Warehouse Request and also the Packing Work Center as can be seen below:

Once the Packing is completed, we can use the Monitor Handling Units app to view the batch details in the Handling Unit.

Once the Warehouse processes are completed the Goods Issue is posted and the stock details for the Batch Managed Product are updated in the S/4 system.

 

Now let's look at the LGM system behavior in the case where we have Missing Batch Details in a Delivery with Batch Managed Product

In this scenario we have a Batch Managed Product but have not provided the batch details in S/4 while creating the delivery. The Delivery is distributed to the S/4 system and is not editable in S/4 system.

Since the product is Batch Managed with missing details, the LGM system validates the data and the Logistical Demand is created with an Error Status as shown below.

To correct the issue, this Logistical Demand can be "Cancelled".

The Cancellation triggers an Integration task to "Decline" the Inbound Delivery in this case which then updates the Delivery in S/4 system.

The system then updates the Good Movement Status to "Completed" with a "Delivered Quantity" as "0".

We can now create a new Inbound Delivery with the required batch details and then transfer the same to LGM correctly with the Batch details.

That's all on the topic of Batch Management in SAP LGM.

Hope this helps.

For More Details on this topic, please refer to the below Links

What's New Viewer - SAP Logistics Management | SAP Help Portal

Batch Management | SAP Help Portal

Batch Replication | SAP Help Portal

Display Batches | SAP Help Portal
